inline_review:
commit 2ee07f5a4fcf25b39aeac61c6b88fe3fdfd0c9b6
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/core: use time_in_range() for damos quota window start

This commit attempts to fix a stall in DAMOS quota charge windows when
a scheme is deactivated for more than LONG_MAX jiffies. It replaces
time_after_eq() with !time_in_range() to properly handle the extended
time wrap-around.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/20260324040722.57944-1-sj@kernel.org

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
> index db6c67e52d2b..8e47183473d3 100644
> --- a/mm/damon/core.c
> +++ b/mm/damon/core.c
> @@ -2452,7 +2452,7 @@ static void damos_adjust_quota(struct damon_ctx *c, struct damos *s)
>  	}
>  
>  	/* New charge window starts */
> -	if (time_after_eq(jiffies, quota->charged_from +
> +	if (!time_in_range(jiffies, quota->charged_from, quota->charged_from +
>  				msecs_to_jiffies(quota->reset_interval))) {

Does this introduce an off-by-one regression that extends every quota charge
window by 1 jiffy?

time_in_range(a, b, c) evaluates the inclusive interval [b, c], which means
it returns true when jiffies exactly equals the end of the interval. The
original code used time_after_eq(), which correctly triggered the new
charge window exactly on that jiffy.

By replacing it with !time_in_range(), the reset evaluates to false on that
exact jiffy and is delayed until the next jiffy. For small reset_interval
configurations or low CONFIG_HZ values, this 1-jiffy delay might alter the
effective quota replenishment rate.

Would using !time_in_range_open() preserve the exact original timing
semantics while still fixing the wrap-around stall issue?

>  		if (damos_quota_is_set(quota) &&
>  				quota->charged_sz >= quota->esz)
logs:
[
  {
    "content": "You are an expert Linux kernel maintainer. Your goal is to perform a deep, rigorous review of a proposed kernel change to ensure safety, performance, and adherence to subsystem standards.

TOOL USAGE: When you need to gather information using tools, actively batch parallel or independent tool calls into a single response to minimize the number of conversation turns.

<global_review_guidelines>
The following documents contain the official technical patterns, architectural rules, and subsystem-specific guidelines that you MUST adhere to during your review. Use these as the absolute source of truth for identifying anti-patterns and violations.

@technical-patterns.md, @timers.md

</global_review_guidelines>


Target Commit:
commit 2ee07f5a4fcf25b39aeac61c6b88fe3fdfd0c9b6
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>
Date:   Sat Mar 28 16:39:29 2026 +0000

    mm/damon/core: use time_in_range() for damos quota window start
    
    damos_adjust_quota() uses time_after_eq() to show if it is time to start
    a new quota charge window, comparing the current jiffies and the
    scheduled next charge window start time.  If it is, the next charge
    window start time is updated and the new charge window starts.
    
    The time check and next window start time update is skipped while the
    scheme is deactivated by the watermarks.  Let's suppose the deactivation
    is kept more than LONG_MAX jiffies (assuming CONFIG_HZ of 250, more than
    99 days in 32 bit systems and more than one billion years in 64 bit
    systems), resulting in having the jiffies larger than the next charge
    window start time + LONG_MAX.  Then, the time_after_eq() call can return
    false until another LONG_MAX jiffies are passed.
    
    This means the scheme can continue working after being reactivated by
    the watermarks.  But, soon, the quota will be exceeded and the scheme
    will again effectively stop working until the next charge window starts.
    Because the current charge window is extended to up to LONG_MAX jiffies,
    however, it will look like it stopped unexpectedly and indefinitely,
    from the user's perspective.
    
    Fix this by using !time_in_range() instead.
    
    The issue was discovered [1] by sashiko.
    
    [1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260324040722.57944-1-sj@kernel.org
    
    Fixes: ee801b7dd782 ("mm/damon/schemes: activate schemes based on a watermarks mechanism")
    Cc: <stable@vger.kernel.org> # 5.16.x
    Signed-off-by: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
index db6c67e52d2b..8e47183473d3 100644
--- a/mm/damon/core.c
+++ b/mm/damon/core.c
@@ -2452,7 +2452,7 @@ static void damos_adjust_quota(struct damon_ctx *c, struct damos *s)
 	}
 
 	/* New charge window starts */
-	if (time_after_eq(jiffies, quota->charged_from +
+	if (!time_in_range(jiffies, quota->charged_from, quota->charged_from +
 				msecs_to_jiffies(quota->reset_interval))) {
 		if (damos_quota_is_set(quota) &&
 				quota->charged_sz >= quota->esz)

      "description": "Using `!time_in_range()` delays the quota reset by 1 jiffy compared to `time_after_eq()`, which alters quota timing and can severely impact small reset intervals.",
      "reasoning": "`time_in_range(a, b, c)` checks if `a` is in the inclusive range `[b, c]`. Therefore, when `jiffies` equals the exact expiration time (`quota->charged_from + interval`), `time_in_range()` evaluates to true, so `!time_in_range()` is false. This causes the code to skip starting the new charge window at the exact expiration jiffy, delaying it until `jiffies > quota->charged_from + interval`. The original code used `time_after_eq()`, which correctly started the window when `jiffies == expiration`. This off-by-one delay alters the quota effective bandwidth; for example, if the reset interval is 1 jiffy, the delay doubles the interval to 2 jiffies, effectively halving the granted quota. The correct macro to preserve the original inclusive/exclusive boundary semantics while fixing the wrap-around is `!time_in_range_open()`."
    }
  ]
}
```",
    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of DAMON Quota Adjustment Patch**

Okay, so I'm diving into this commit that's modifying `damos_adjust_quota` within the DAMON core. The patch's aim is to handle a scenario where DAMON's scheme gets deactivated for a long stretch (longer than `LONG_MAX` jiffies), and upon reactivation, the current `jiffies` could potentially be *way* beyond the expected charging window.

My initial thought is to trace the flow and spot what the original code did versus the new implementation. The old code used `time_after_eq`, which seems straightforward for comparing the current time (`jiffies`) against the window's end.  The new approach is using `!time_in_range()`, I have to really think about this. It is defined as a combination of `time_after_eq` and `time_before_eq`. Expanding this out, I can see that `!time_in_range(jiffies, charged_from, charged_from + interval)` should be equivalent to `jiffies < charged_from || jiffies > charged_from + interval`.

I started to compare both blocks and my first concern arose.  The new code, with its use of `!time_in_range`, subtly alters the condition when a new charging window starts.  Specifically, when `jiffies` *exactly* matches the end of the current interval, the original code with `time_after_eq` would trigger the reset. The new code, however, will *not* trigger the reset, delaying it by exactly 1 jiffy.  This is a critical off-by-one error and could be significant if the `reset_interval` is small.

I carefully considered the wrap-around scenario. If the system is dormant for too long (past `LONG_MAX`), then the typical jiffies comparisons can be a problem. This is exactly what the author of the commit is trying to correct.  I confirmed that the new code *does* handle the wrap-around case, correctly resetting when `jiffies` is, in effect, much further than the original window end. If the condition is to catch values before and after, the original code had a limitation because comparing `time_after_eq` could not accurately assess that `jiffies` passed the charging window end because of the unsigned comparison for very large `jiffies` values. The new code is indeed safer and better for the overflow.

But the 1 jiffy delay remains. And the more I think about it, this change might not be the best solution. The author's goal of "if `jiffies` is inside the current window, do not reset. If it's outside the current window, reset" is correct, but there is a more accurate macro to do that and not have an edge case.  There's a better macro for the inclusive/exclusive boundaries: `time_in_range_open`, which tests for the range `[b, c)`. Using `!time_in_range_open` would perfectly mimic the existing, original logic. The key difference is whether to use the inclusive or exclusive check. Because of the behavior in `time_in_range` and `time_in_range_open`, the reset will delay by 1 jiffy if not corrected.

In summary, the commit *does* fix a potential wrap-around issue. But its choice of `!time_in_range` introduces an off-by-one jiffy delay. For small intervals, this can lead to substantial (up to 100%) changes in the effective window, and the quota is applied for a longer time which impacts the performance.  Using `!time_in_range_open` would provide a more correct and accurate fix, matching the original code's behavior. The current commit alters the behavior.

      "reasoning": "The previous code used `time_after_eq(jiffies, quota->charged_from + interval)`, which evaluates to `true` exactly when `jiffies == charged_from + interval`. The new code uses `!time_in_range(jiffies, quota->charged_from, quota->charged_from + interval)`. Because `time_in_range()` uses inclusive bounds for both start and end (`time_after_eq` and `time_before_eq`), it evaluates to `true` when `jiffies` exactly equals `charged_from + interval`. As a result, `!time_in_range()` evaluates to `false` at that exact tick, delaying the start of the next charge window until `jiffies == charged_from + interval + 1`. This effectively extends every quota charge window by 1 jiffy, which could have a large proportional impact on small reset intervals (e.g., doubling the length of a 1-jiffy interval). Replacing this with `!time_in_range_open()`, which represents a half-open interval `[start, end)`, would preserve the original exact boundary semantics while still properly fixing the > LONG_MAX wrap-around stall case."
    }

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Analysis of Charge Window Logic Change**

Okay, so this commit changes how we determine when to start a new charge window in `damos_adjust_quota()`. The original code used `time_after_eq`, which seems straightforward enough. Now it's been swapped with `!time_in_range()`, and my initial thought is, "Hm, interesting, why?"

`time_in_range(a, b, c)` checks if `a` falls within the inclusive range of `[b, c]`. So, `!time_in_range()` means "not in this range," which should work. Initially, when `jiffies` is bigger than the start time `charged_from` of a charge window, `time_after_eq` would be true and things would proceed. If `jiffies` is within the range of the current window `charged_from` to `charged_from + reset_interval`, `time_in_range` is true, and `!time_in_range` is false. However, there's a crucial consideration: what if `jiffies` wraps around?

If `jiffies` wraps around, so that it's much larger than the end of the current window, it may *seem* "before" the start of the window in terms of time, but is actually in the future, if we consider `jiffies` to be an ever-increasing time counter. The commit's message correctly points out a scenario where a long deactivation of the system could cause `jiffies` to become larger than the next expected charge window start time plus `LONG_MAX`. That's where the old code might fail.

Let's say `charged_from` is 0 and `reset_interval` is 100. If `jiffies` jumps to `LONG_MAX + 200`, the old `time_after_eq` would evaluate to false, meaning no new charge window, while `!time_in_range()` would be true, which is the desired behavior. The commit intends to solve that specific edge case. So far, so good.

Now, here's where my expert instincts start to kick in: this commit introduces a subtle off-by-one behavior change. Let's trace it carefully. If `jiffies` equals the end of a charge window `charged_from + reset_interval`, `time_in_range()` evaluates to *true*. Thus, `!time_in_range` evaluates to *false*, which means a *new* window *won't* start yet. But, the old code, using `time_after_eq(jiffies, charged_from + reset_interval)` would have evaluated to true for this boundary condition, hence the new window would start when `jiffies` equals `charged_from + reset_interval`. This is one jiffy delay.

The `time_in_range` macro is inclusive of both ends. This means the start of the *next* charge window is delayed by one jiffy as the test is not true until `jiffies > charged_from + reset_interval`. It appears to be an unintended consequence of the switch. This off-by-one matters, especially for small `reset_interval` values. If `reset_interval` maps to just a few jiffies, it could significantly alter the quota period by a noticeable percentage.

The right thing to do to address the problem described in the commit message *without* changing the current behavior, is to use `time_in_range_open`, where the test is exclusive of the end of the range. `time_in_range_open` captures exactly the same wrap-around semantics, *and* preserves the original boundary condition.

I’ll document that off-by-one error, as it's a regression in behavior, even if it might be considered minor in many cases. The commit has correctly identified an issue with the wrap-around, but it introduced a small error in the process.

    {
      "type": "Logic Error / Off-by-one",
      "description": "The use of !time_in_range() introduces an off-by-one error that elongates every quota charge window by at least 1 jiffy.",
      "reasoning": "1. `time_in_range(a, b, c)` checks the inclusive interval `[b, c]`, meaning it returns true when `a == c`.\n2. The original code used `time_after_eq(jiffies, next_window_start)`, which triggered the new charge window EXACTLY when `jiffies == next_window_start`.\n3. By using `!time_in_range()`, the condition evaluates to false when `jiffies == next_window_start`, causing the reset to be skipped until `jiffies > next_window_start`.\n4. Since `quota->charged_from` is set to the current `jiffies` when the reset finally occurs, this artificially extends the duration of every charge window by at least 1 jiffy, systematically reducing the quota replenishment rate over time.\n5. The correct fix that preserves the exact original timing semantics while fixing the `LONG_MAX` wrap-around issue is to use `!time_in_range_open()`, which treats the upper bound `c` as exclusive."

    {
      "type": "Data Loss / Overflow / Truncation",
      "description": "Lack of bounds checking on `quota->reset_interval` before passing to `msecs_to_jiffies()` causes truncation on 64-bit systems and macro breakage on 32-bit systems.",
      "reasoning": "1. `quota->reset_interval` is defined as `unsigned long`, but it is passed to `msecs_to_jiffies()`, which takes an `unsigned int`. On 64-bit systems, a value greater than `UINT_MAX` results in silent 32-bit truncation (e.g., yielding a near-zero interval).\n2. On 32-bit systems, `msecs_to_jiffies()` can return values up to `UINT_MAX`, which exceeds `LONG_MAX` (the maximum safe delta for jiffies macros). If the interval exceeds `LONG_MAX` jiffies (approx. 24.8 days at HZ=1000), the signed arithmetic in `time_in_range()` overflows.\n3. When this overflow occurs, `time_in_range()` continuously evaluates to false, causing `!time_in_range()` to evaluate to true, resetting the quota immediately on every tick and completely disabling the DAMOS quota limitations.\n4. The interval must be validated to safely fit within `unsigned int` and not exceed `LONG_MAX` jiffies."
    }
  ]
}

    "role": "assistant",
    "thought": "**Deep Dive into DAMON Subsystem Patch**

Okay, so I'm looking at this patch in `mm/damon/core.c`, specifically the `damos_adjust_quota()` function, and something's immediately jumping out at me. The core of this patch seems to be changing the condition that determines when a new quota charge window starts. The old code used `time_after_eq()`, but now it's using `!time_in_range()`. Let's break this down: `time_in_range(a, b, c)` means `a` is within the range `[b, c]`. The `!` negates that. So, the *new* code triggers the start of a new window if the current time (`jiffies`) is *outside* the window. However, the original code triggers exactly at the window boundary.

This is where things get interesting. If the window boundary is *included* in the `time_in_range` calculation, the new condition causes a delay. It means the new window doesn't begin precisely when it should. Instead, it begins at the *next* time step. Let's see... If `jiffies` is exactly `quota->charged_from + interval`, then `time_in_range` is TRUE, so `!time_in_range` is FALSE! The patch is pushing the boundary by a jiffy. I can see what they're *trying* to do, address this `LONG_MAX` issue where `jiffies` has potentially wrapped around... but it looks like a one-jiffy delay is a regression.

Let's trace out `time_in_range`. Original condition was `if (time_after_eq(jiffies, quota->charged_from + interval))`, meaning we trigger when `jiffies == charged_from + interval`. Now the new condition is `!time_in_range`. So, the window reset is delayed by one jiffy! If the window should trigger at 100, the *new* code won't trigger until 101! So, with a 100ms reset window, this is a clear doubling of the effective reset interval in the worst case! If the sampling is 1 jiffy, it doubles the length! That's a huge logic error. The commit message alludes to `LONG_MAX`, but is the behavior change intended?

The fix, in this case, would have been to use `!time_in_range_open()`. `time_in_range_open(a, b, c)` checks if `a` is in the open interval `[b, c)`, so it is exactly equivalent to `time_after_eq()`. That would exactly match the original code's behavior *and* fix the intended LONG_MAX overflow. This is a clear bug I have found with the new code!

But that's not the only thing. The code calls `msecs_to_jiffies(quota->reset_interval)`. `reset_interval` is an `unsigned long`, and `msecs_to_jiffies` takes an `unsigned int`! If `reset_interval` is a huge number (e.g., set via sysfs), it'll get silently truncated on 64-bit systems! So the intended interval is changed to another completely different interval, leading to unexpected behavior. This has a massive implication! If the user gives an invalid `reset_interval` value, it's silently truncated, and all kinds of problems arise.

Also, on 32-bit systems, where `UINT_MAX` is larger than `LONG_MAX`, if the interval is *large* (but still smaller than 49 days) there's an even bigger problem. If interval > LONG_MAX, we can encounter a case where the jiffies arithmetic in the range check simply breaks, resulting in immediate resets on EVERY TICK!

So, the concerns are (1) a subtle, but significant, off-by-one error introduced with `!time_in_range()` which *increases* window length by 1 jiffy compared to `time_after_eq()`; (2) Potential silent data loss and macro arithmetic breakage and overflow when large values are passed as quota parameters, which can effectively DISABLE quota enforcement. The code should use `!time_in_range_open` to preserve original behavior, or at least document if this is intentional, and there must be bounds-checking!

After carefully reviewing the code and comparing the old logic (`time_after_eq`) with the new (`!time_in_range()`), I've found a critical issue.

Specifically, the use of `!time_in_range()` introduces a 1-jiffy off-by-one error that extends every quota charge window by one jiffy. Let me break down the logic:

1.  The `time_in_range(a, b, c)` function, by definition, evaluates the *inclusive* interval \[b, c]. This means it returns `true` when `a` is equal to `c`.
2.  The original code used `time_after_eq(jiffies, quota->charged_from + interval)`. This was designed to trigger the start of the *new* charge window *exactly* when `jiffies` equaled `charged_from + interval`. This ensures that the window length will be exactly the intended interval.
3.  By replacing `time_after_eq` with `!time_in_range()`, the critical point where the new window should trigger is shifted. The condition now evaluates to `false` at the *exact* time (`jiffies == charged_from + interval`), *delaying* the reset until `jiffies` becomes greater than `charged_from + interval`. This ensures that the window is extended by 1 jiffy.
4.  Consequently, this 1-jiffy delay artificially extends the duration of *every* charge window, making the effective quota replenished rate slower. The impact of this delay is small, but if the interval is only a few jiffies, it causes a significant relative difference. For example, if the initial rate is 10 ms, the new rate will be slowed down to 11 ms. This difference will continue to stack up over time, which can lead to a significant quota throttling.
5.  I want to highlight that this issue wasn't a conceptual misunderstanding or a simple mistake, but a logic error. Using `!time_in_range_open()`, which treats the upper bound as *exclusive*, would have preserved the exact original timing semantics *while* still correctly handling the `LONG_MAX` wrap-around issue the original commit aimed to address.

I am assigning this issue a **Medium** severity. While this logic error leads to incorrect timing, I don't believe it will crash the system, cause data loss, or cause significant performance degradation. However, it's a measurable behavior change, a logic error in quota management, and it could lead to incorrect throttling if the quota interval is low.     "content": "commit 2ee07f5a4fcf25b39aeac61c6b88fe3fdfd0c9b6
Author: SeongJae Park <sj@kernel.org>

mm/damon/core: use time_in_range() for damos quota window start

This commit attempts to fix a stall in DAMOS quota charge windows when
a scheme is deactivated for more than LONG_MAX jiffies. It replaces
time_after_eq() with !time_in_range() to properly handle the extended
time wrap-around.

Link: https://lore.kernel.org/20260324040722.57944-1-sj@kernel.org

> diff --git a/mm/damon/core.c b/mm/damon/core.c
